description: The GPIO module is clock-gated after reset and remains inactive until explicitly enabled. Enabling is performed through the Power and Sleep Controller (PSC) and its local PSC (LPSC) state machine for the GPIO module. Software must execute the module enable sequence and poll/verify the transitioned state before any register access that affects pins. Accessing GPIO registers prior to enable has no external effect and can mislead bring-up diagnostics. Perform this step early in initialization to make subsequent configuration deterministic. Keep the enable order consistent across boots to ease debug and reproducibility.
evidence: PSC enable sequence; LPSC for GPIO module
dependencies: PSC domain ON; LPSC for GPIO transitioned to ENABLE
Remarks: Always verify the final PSC state and handle errors before proceeding to pinmux and DIR configuration.

feature_name: Pin multiplexing selection for GPIO pins
description: Many device pins are multiplexed among alternate functions and do not default to GPIO. The pin multiplexing (pinmux) control must route the desired package pins to the GPIO function before direction or data is set. Applying pinmux first avoids driving unintended peripherals and prevents latch-up or bus contention. Board constraints and schematic mappings should guide which pins are safe to switch to GPIO. Configure only the pins actually needed and leave others at their reset-safe function. Re-read back pinmux settings to confirm the intended map before I/O becomes active.
dependencies: Pinmux control registers configured; board constraints applied
Remarks: Stage pinmux configuration before DIR/data programming and document board-specific mappings for maintainability.

description: Each GPIO bit can be individually configured as input or output via the direction register (DIR). Program outputs only after pinmux and power are valid to avoid glitches at the pad. For inputs, enable any required pull configuration externally or via board design if applicable. When switching a pin from output to input, drive it to a benign level first to minimize transients. Masked writes and read-back verification help ensure only targeted bits are affected. Keep a cached shadow of DIR if multiple contexts update direction.
dependencies: GPIO clock enabled; pinmux set for targeted pins
Remarks: Batch direction updates logically to reduce ISR contention and validate with a read-back compare in debug builds.

description: Use the dedicated SET_DATA and CLR_DATA registers for atomic modification of output bits. These registers avoid read-modify-write hazards that can occur when multiple contexts access the GPIO port. Prefer atomic updates over full-port OUTDATA writes for partial bit changes. This improves determinism and prevents unintended toggles under interrupt preemption. Maintain masks for groups of outputs to simplify safe multi-bit sequences. Verify the intended level with a non-destructive read when timing allows.
dependencies: DIR configured for outputs; GPIO clock enabled
Remarks: Avoid OUTDATA RMW under concurrency; rely on atomic paths and short critical sections only when absolutely necessary.

description: The INDATA register provides synchronized snapshots of the GPIO input levels. Sampling should apply bit masks to read only the pins of interest and prevent spurious interpretation. Because inputs are synchronized, expect a small and bounded latency relative to the external signal. Polling intervals should account for debounce and external hardware filtering as needed. For high-rate events, prefer interrupts or DMA-triggered capture over tight polling loops. Validate input polarity against board wiring before deployment.
dependencies: GPIO clock enabled; pins configured as inputs
Remarks: Introduce debouncing in software or hardware for mechanical inputs and document expected active level per signal.

description: Enable per-pin rising-edge detection using the SET_RIS_TRIG register and disable using CLR_RIS_TRIG. Clear any latched status prior to enable to avoid spurious first interrupts. Configure routing to the desired CPU vector before unmasking to maintain a safe servicing path. Ensure ISR latency and work budget can handle possible burst arrivals. Periodically verify that status clearing semantics are functioning as expected under stress. Keep edge enables tightly scoped to the pins in active use only.
dependencies: Interrupt routing prepared; status registers cleared
Remarks: Always clear stale status before enabling edges and confirm the ISR clears both module and CPU flags.

description: Enable per-pin falling-edge detection using the SET_FAL_TRIG register and disable using CLR_FAL_TRIG. As with rising edges, clear any pre-existing status to prevent false triggers at activation. Coordinate edge polarity with external circuitry so the intended transition is captured. Route the event through the interrupt infrastructure before unmasking to ensure a valid ISR path. Validate behavior with controlled stimuli across temperature and voltage corners. Keep documentation of which signals are active-low for future maintenance.
dependencies: Interrupt routing prepared; status registers cleared
Remarks: Match edge polarity to board signalling and test with both single and burst transitions during bring-up.

description: Per-pin status latches capture edge events until cleared by software using a write-one-to-clear (W1C) semantic. Reading status identifies which pins asserted and in what combination. Clearing serviced bits promptly prevents re-entry and lost events under high rates. Implement a deterministic order for read/clear to avoid race conditions. Coordinate with CPU flag handling so both module and core-level flags are serviced. Confirm no unintended clears occur when writing adjacent bits.
dependencies: GPIO status registers accessible; CPU IFR handling in place
Remarks: Use a strict read-modify-write mask for W1C writes and verify end-to-end in ISR unit tests.

description: GPIO pins are organized into banks that aggregate interrupt lines to the CPU. On an interrupt, first read the bank status and then resolve down to per-pin status to find exact sources. Multiple pins within a bank may assert simultaneously and must be handled without loss. Ensure the ISR loops through all active bits before exit to avoid missing events. Balance bank size and vector usage according to system latency and throughput goals. Provide back-pressure or event coalescing if software cannot keep up.
evidence: GPIO bank interrupt status/enable; aggregated INT line
dependencies: Interrupt mux configured; bank line mapped to CPU INTx
Remarks: Design ISRs to drain all active sources per entry and instrument service time to prevent overruns.

description: Some devices support routing individual GPIO pins directly to distinct CPU interrupt lines. Selecting direct routing can reduce identification overhead and improve latency when vectors are available. Availability and exact mappings depend on the specific device configuration. Evaluate vector budget and prioritize latency-critical signals for direct mapping if supported. Fall back to banked aggregation when vectors are constrained. Document any per-pin direct routes so maintenance does not inadvertently remap them.
evidence: Interrupt mapping tables; per-pin routing (availability UNKNOWN)
dependencies: Interrupt mapping tables applied; availability device-specific (UNKNOWN)
Remarks: Confirm hardware support before relying on direct routes and gate enablement behind device-detection logic.

description: GPIO interrupt outputs are routed through the device interrupt multiplexer to a chosen CPU vector. Correct mux programming ensures events reach the intended core line and can be serviced. Enable the CPU vector and configure priority and masking as appropriate for the system. In the ISR, clear both the module status and any CPU-level flags to complete servicing. Validate end-to-end by injecting known edges and observing vector hits. Keep mux configuration centralized to avoid conflicting ownership across modules.
dependencies: Interrupt mux configured; CPU IER/IFR managed
Remarks: Centralize interrupt mux ownership and provide diagnostics to trace event paths during debug.

feature_name: EDMA event generation from GPIO edges
description: Selected GPIO edge events can be mapped through an event crossbar to trigger EDMA transfers. This offloads high-rate or low-latency signaling paths from the CPU. Use for tasks such as timestamp capture or buffered sampling without tight polling. Ensure the EDMA channel configuration matches event pacing and buffer depth. Coordinate clearing of GPIO status with EDMA completion to prevent missed triggers. Validate throughput and error paths under sustained loads.
evidence: EDMA/event crossbar mapping from GPIO edges
dependencies: EDMA present; event crossbar mapping configured
Remarks: Size EDMA buffers for worst-case burst lengths and include error-handling on transfer completion callbacks.

description: GPIO inputs traverse synchronizer flip-flops that stabilize asynchronous signals. This introduces a small pipeline latency between external level changes and INDATA observations. During debug halts, synchronizers preserve signal integrity but can shift apparent timing. Account for this latency in software that timestamps input changes. Avoid making assumptions about sub-cycle timing at the pin without external measurement. Document expected propagation so validation teams can reconcile scope traces with software reads.
dependencies: GPIO clock stable; external signal integrity ensured
Remarks: Include synchronizer latency in timing budgets and avoid cycle-accurate assumptions in polling code.

description: A robust sequence is: enable PSC for GPIO, configure pinmux, program DIR for intended roles, set initial outputs atomically, clear latched status, and then enable required edges and routes. This ordering minimizes unintended glitches and spurious events. Read back key registers after each phase to confirm state. Keep the sequence idempotent so it can be safely re-run after subsystem resets. Encapsulate the flow in a single routine with clear preconditions and postconditions. Log failures with enough context to aid bring-up and field diagnostics.
evidence: PSC enable; pinmux; DIR; status clear sequence
dependencies: PSC enabled; pinmux set; DIR programmed; status cleared
Remarks: Implement the sequence as a reusable routine and guard it with assertions in debug builds.

feature_name: Concurrent updates and RMW hazard avoidance
description: Concurrent access to GPIO outputs can create read-modify-write hazards if using full-port writes. Prefer atomic SET/CLR paths and guard any unavoidable full-port writes with short critical sections. Coordinate access between tasks and ISRs using masks and clear ownership. Where feasible, queue output updates and coalesce them to reduce contention. Continuously measure ISR and task timing to ensure updates meet deadlines. Provide fallbacks when preemption frequency increases under load.
evidence: Atomic SET/CLR vs OUTDATA RMW behavior
dependencies: Atomic SET/CLR available; ISR/task access coordinated
Remarks: Adopt atomic-only update APIs and restrict direct OUTDATA access to controlled, single-owner contexts.

description: At reset the GPIO module's clock is disabled, so register writes and reads may not reflect external behavior until enabled. Software must not assume any default pin state is driven to the pad. Always bring the module to an enabled and known state before interacting with external circuitry. Verify the enable took effect by reading back a reliable indicator or status. This practice prevents subtle bring-up issues and protects attached components. Keep this behavior documented in system initialization notes.
evidence: Reset state clock-gated; PSC module state
dependencies: PSC enable completed; module state verified
Remarks: Gate all GPIO interactions on a verified-enabled state and include safeguards in early boot code.}
